summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the principal application is part-heard – Where the applicant filed and served a notice to produce on the respondent – Where the documents subject of the notice to produce are subject to legal professional privilege – Where the applicant asserts that the respondent impliedly waived privilege by acting inconsistent with the maintenance of the confidentiality of the communications – Where most of the contents of the communications were not put in issue and are not open to scrutiny -Where the respondent made partial disclosure of communications between himself and his lawyer to the applicant – Where the respondent waived privilege for the relevant communication.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J

The applicant and the respondent are engaged in litigation over their property interests. Their competing applications are listed before another judge of this court for the continuation of a trial that commenced some time ago.
The applicant served a Notice to Produce upon the respondent on 30 October 2025. By an Application in a Proceeding filed on 17 November 2025 the respondent seeks, amongst other relief, an order setting aside the Notice to Produce in its entirety. The applicant opposes that relief and seeks particular orders for the production of certain documents otherwise within the scope of the Notice to Produce.
This application came on for hearing on 1 December 2025 before the judge before whom the trial is presently part-heard. On 12 December 2025 her Honour made orders that dealt with certain aspects of the Application in a Proceeding and the Response to it but, by paragraph 4 of that order (as amended on 13 January 2026), the respondent’s application for items 2, 3 and 4 of the Notice to Produce to be set aside on the grounds of legal professional privilege, and the relief sought in paragraph 4 of the applicant’s amended Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 3 December 2025 for the production of the documents specified therein, was listed before me for hearing. That course was adopted so as to avoid any possibility that the trial judge might become compromised by reason of having to consider privileged material for the purposes of the respondent’s application.

The applicant would like to see the respondent’s instructions to his lawyers

Relevantly, the Notice to Produce seeks the production of all communications between the respondent, or any person on his behalf, and his lawyers in Country B, JJ Legal Services, for the period from l August 2022 to 26 March 2023. It also seeks production of the entirety of the communications or correspondence from Damien Greer Lawyers referred to “in the message from the Respondent dated 1 February 2024 (referred to in the Applicant’s affidavit dated 18 March 2025 at paragraph 176.2 and at annexure 179)”.
Paragraph 4 of the amended Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 3 December 2025 particularises some further documents the applicant seeks to inspect. There is some overlap between those documents and those set out in the Notice to Produce.
At the outset of the hearing before me, senior counsel for the applicant confirmed that the orders sought by the applicant are those set out in Part E of his client’s case outline filed on 29 December 2025. Those orders particularise the documents sought to be inspected by the applicant and which fall within paragraphs 2, 3 and 4 of the Notice to Produce and paragraph 4 of the amended Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 3 December 2025.

The context

To understand these requests, some context is necessary.
On 20 July 2022, the applicant and the respondent entered into a financial agreement pursuant to s 90C of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth). They were then married to each other and their relationship was, ostensibly, intact.
The applicant claims however, that the financial agreement should be set aside for, amongst other things, fraud, pursuant to s 90K of the Act. The fraud relied upon by the applicant arises because, she claims, the respondent represented that the marriage would continue if she signed the financial agreement and that he wished to continue to be married to her. She says she executed the agreement because of these representations.
However, the applicant says that only days after signing the financial agreement, the respondent began to say that he wanted to divorce her. She argues that given that these statements came so soon after the signing of the financial agreement and in the absence of some precipitating event between them, the only logical conclusion to draw is that the respondent never intended to remain in a relationship with her or to remain married to her. His statements to her to the contrary must have been knowingly false.
The respondent says that the applicant’s case must fail because he was not fraudulent. He did not want the parties’ marriage to end when the agreement was signed. However, he claims that after the financial agreement was signed, he discovered that the applicant was pursuing a relationship with another man. That discovery came a few weeks after the agreement was signed when he received seven text messages not meant for him, but another. He claims that this immediately led him to resolve to bring his relationship with and marriage to the applicant to an end. The applicant denies any infidelity or sending the subject text messages at all.
At this point it is sufficient to record that there is some objective evidence that demonstrates that the relationship between the applicant and the respondent had not always been rosy. There is evidence of some communications between the respondent and one or other of his employees with some lawyers in Country B, JJ Legal Services, relating to prospective divorce proceedings between the applicant and the respondent as early as May 2021. There is also evidence of communications between the respondent and one or other of his employees on his behalf, and these Country B lawyers in September 2022 about the respondent instituting divorce proceedings in Country B. Divorce proceedings were in fact commenced by the respondent in late 2022 in Country B. The application nominates a date of 21 May 2021 as the date of the parties’ separation. The reasons given for the divorce do not include the applicant’s infidelity. These aspects of the content of the divorce application, presumably prepared by the Country B lawyers on instructions from the respondent, are at odds with the case that he now pursues.
I will deal with these communications, and the Country B divorce application, in a little more detail later in these reasons as they are the documents to which the applicant seeks access.

The instructions to the Country B lawyers are privileged

Each of the documents sought by the applicant are said to record the respondent’s instructions to his lawyers. All, save for the last document (item 4 in the Notice to Produce), are communications to or from the respondent’s Country B lawyers about the divorce proceedings in Country B. Item 4 in the notice to produce is a communication from the respondent’s Australian lawyers (those on the record for him in these proceedings) to him. It was common ground that each of the documents sought to be inspected by the applicant were properly the subject of a claim of legal professional privilege at common law.
This is not a case where the privilege provided by s 118 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) is engaged, there being no application to adduce evidence caught by the statutory privilege.
The question on this application is whether, in respect of the relevant documents, the respondent has waived his common law privilege against producing them for inspection by the applicant.

The loss of legal professional privilege

The applicant argues that, notwithstanding that the Evidence Act has no application here, the common law test to be applied to determine whether privilege in the relevant documents has been lost is the same as that set out in s 122(2) of the Evidence Act. However, the provisions of s 122 as to the circumstances in which privilege may be lost are not identical to the corresponding common law principles (Mann v Carnell [1999] HCA 66; (1999) 201 CLR 1 at [23]; Osland v Secretary, Department of Justice (2008) 234 CLR 275 at [49]).
The test to be applied in the instant case, is described by the High Court in Mann v Carnell at [29], is as follows:
Waiver may be express or implied. Disputes as to implied waiver usually arise from the need to decide whether particular conduct is inconsistent with the maintenance of the confidentiality which the privilege is intended to protect.
Thus the question here is whether in respect of each particular document or group of documents, there is any particular conduct on the part of the respondent that can be said to be inconsistent with the maintenance of the confidentiality of his instructions to his lawyers or their advice (in the case of the last document) to him.
A short summary of each of the documents or groups of documents sought to be inspected (as identified in the orders on Part E of the applicant’s case outline filed on 12 December 2025) is useful.

Email from Mr TT to the respondent dated 5 September 2022

This email is referred to by both the respondent in his affidavit filed on 30 May 2025 (annexed as Annexure MRV-3) and the affidavit of his personal assistant, Mr TT, filed on 30 May 2025 (annexed as Annexure MRTT-3).
This is an email in which the respondent provides instructions about a divorce application in Country B to his lawyers there. Mr TT says that he prepared the email in the presence of the respondent and on the respondent’s instructions. A copy of the annexure from each affidavit is before me. They are, for all relevant purposes, identical. Apart from two introductory sentences:
Below is what I have drafted at your request to send to [Mr UU]. If there is a specific format that it needs to be in, let me know and I can make changes and add or remove anything from this.

and a concluding sentence:

[Mr Villa] please let me know if you would like me to add any other information here or make it shorter.

the seven paragraphs that form the body of the email are redacted.
The person named Mr UU, I was told, was the respondent’s inhouse lawyer.

Email from the respondent to JJ Legal Services dated 7 September 2022

This email was sent by the respondent to his Country B lawyer on 7 September 2022. Its genesis was the draft prepared by Mr TT and referred to above. All of its content is redacted in the copy of the document that appears as Annexure MRV-4 to the respondent’s affidavit filed on 30 May 2025.

A copy of the document or documents evidencing instructions provided by the respondent to JJ Legal Services referred to at paragraph 9 of the respondent’s affidavit filed 30 May 2025.

All paragraph 9 of the respondent’s affidavit filed on 30 May 2025 says is:
In or around late August 2022, I instructed my lawyers in [Country B] [JJ Legal Services]. [JJ Legal Services] in relation to my separation and subsequent divorce from my former wife, [Ms Villa].

All communications between the respondent and any person acting on his behalf and JJ Legal Services in relation to any actual or possible divorce or separation from the applicant in the period from August 2022 to March 2023 (being the period referred to at paragraphs 278 to 287 of the affidavit of Ms Villa affirmed 6 February 2025).

These paragraphs in the applicant’s affidavit filed on 7 February 2025 set out what she says were her dealings and communications with the respondent between early August 2022 when she says he told her that he was going to Country B to arrange their divorce and March 2023 when she received a copy of the divorce orders from the respondent.

The instructions to the respondent’s lawyer in Country B referred to in paragraph 244 of the respondent’s affidavit filed 1 March 2025.

In this paragraph, the respondent swears that in late 2022, he instructed his lawyer in Country B to file the divorce application at the Family Court in City VV.

The instructions given by the respondent’s personal assistant to the Country B lawyers referred to in paragraph 245 of the respondent’s affidavit filed 1 March 2025.

Referring to the divorce application that was filed in the Family Court in City VV, this paragraph provides:
Paragraph 8 of that application indicates that [Ms Villa] and I have been separated since 26 May 2021. This is not correct. This is the date that my assistant approached [Mr WW] about a divorce in [Country B] but is not our date of separation. To the best of my knowledge the reason for this date was the date my assistant [Ms KK] first contacted [Mr WW] to give instructions to commence an application for divorce.

The instructions given by the respondent to his personal assistant, enabling him to provide the instructions to the Country B lawyer referred to in paragraphs 244 and 245 of the respondent’s affidavit filed 1 March 2025.

The documents to which this description applies is illusory. The respondent does not refer to any instructions given by him to his personal assistant, enabling his personal assistant to provide the instructions to the Country B lawyer in paragraphs 244 and 245 of his affidavit filed on 30 May 2025.

The entirety of the text message dated 1 February 2024 referred to in the applicants’ affidavit dated 18 March 2025 annexure 179

In paragraph 176 – 177 of the applicant’s affidavit filed on 18 March 2025, she swears:
In response to paragraph 318 of [Mr Villa’s] Affidavit:
176.1 Since my lawyers in these proceedings first wrote to [Mr Villa] in July 2024 to perform his obligations under the Financial Agreement, he has never expressed any such “willingness”

176.2 On 1 February 2024 [Mr Villa] sent me some messages containing part of a letter, apparently from his lawyers. Annexed and marked “[MSV-179]” Is a copy of that screenshot. Underneath, in [Country D language], [Mr Villa] and I exchanged some messages as follows (my translation):

[MR VILLA]:

[in English] You suggest the balance funds due to [Ms Villa] be held on her behalf by a trustee. We do not advise that you adopt such approach, in view of the risk set out above. If the remainder funds are to be held in the trust, then we suggest that it be held on [Ms Villa’s] behalf by an unrelated trustee, with [Ms Villa] specifically consent to the appointment of the trustee to hold the funds on her behalf.

Yours faithfully

DAMIEN GREER LAWYERS [4:18pm]

[MR VILLA]:

1) I copied the lawyer’s answer relevant to your question.

2) I did not copy and am not sending you the previous paragraphs, as they are not relevant to your question.

3) If you want to personally appoint me responsible for the storage of funds for some time and receive some “interest” monthly, you will need to give me written consent and instructions.

4) In any case, whoever you entrust or instruct - you can / must oblige the trusted person to return the funds to you within, say, a month, three, half a year after your written request.

5) I hope this answer is sufficient for you. [4:24 pm]

[MS VILLA]: Thank you. Understood [4:27pm]

[MS VILLA]: What is approximate percent? [4:27pm]

[MR VILLA]:Today in USD I think 5%[4:28pm]

[MR VILLA]: Pay attention to the word “I think” [4:29pm]

[MR VILLA]: and pay attention that I said it from Myself. I don’t know what will decided another person in trust...[4:30pm]

[MR VILLA]: More of that... you showed me your distrust many times

Long time ago I wanted to say that you are not right. I’m a decent

person. And time will prove you that. [4:32 pm]

NOTE: [Country D] slang for not happy with him.

[MR VILLA]: and [Mr HH] is decent. I need to tell this to you. Even lately you are rolling a barrel on him

(are not happy with him). he is not responsible in your desiase. Like me. [4:56 pm]

(faithfully reproduced, emoji omitted)

The respondent has not waived the privilege in the instructions to his Country B lawyers

Leaving aside the last document, to which I shall return shortly, the evidence relied upon by the applicant to establish the necessary waiver of privilege does nothing more than state the fact that the respondent, or one of his personal assistants, gave instructions to his Country B lawyers. To state that and nothing more goes nowhere towards disclosing the confidential content of the instructions. Nor is it conduct which is inconsistent with the maintenance of the confidentiality of his instructions.
To be fair, the applicant did not suggest that there had been some waiver of the privilege because of some disclosure of the confidential information contained within the relevant communications. Rather, the applicant’s argument is that because:
(a) the respondent wishes to advance a case that he pursued his divorce application because he found out about the applicant’s infidelity after the financial agreement was signed;

(b) the object evidence tends to suggest that the respondent was contemplating divorcing before the agreement was signed; and

(c) the respondent commenced proceedings for divorce but did not mention the applicant’s infidelity as a reason for the divorce

the respondent has acted in a way that is inconsistent with the maintenance of the confidentiality of his communications with his Country B lawyers.

The applicant argues that the reason for the respondent separating from the applicant and divorcing her is central to the case. By putting in issue his reason for divorcing the applicant, the applicant argues that, to put it in terms stated by Allsop J in DSE (Holdings) Pty Ltd v Intertan Inc [2003] FCA 384; (2003) 127 FCR 499 at 519 [58], the respondent makes an assertion, or brings a case, which is either about the contents of the confidential communication or which necessarily lays open the confidential communication to scrutiny. She argues that by such conduct, an inconsistency arises between the act (of propounding the case that he does) and the maintenance of the confidence, informed partly by the forensic unfairness of allowing the claim to proceed without disclosure of the communication.
However, I cannot accept that argument. I do not accept that by taking issue with the respondent about the reason and timing for his decision to divorce the applicant, he has acted in a way that is inconsistent with the maintenance of the confidentiality of his instructions to his Country B lawyers. The case is not about the content of the communications that he has had with his lawyers about the relevant issue. Nor does he open those instructions to scrutiny simply because he takes a position that is at odds with that of the applicant.
The applicant sought to draw support for her argument from the decision of the Full Court of the Family Court of Australia in Stamp v Stamp [2007] FamCA 420; (2007) 37 Fam LR 235, but nothing in that case assists her argument. At issue there was the wife’s capacity to give instructions to her lawyers. It is hardly surprising then, that the court considered that any privilege that subsisted over her solicitors’ file and her communications with those solicitors had been waived by the wife raising that issue.
The applicant also sought to draw support from the decision at first instance in Sterling & Sterling (2021) FamCA 575. In that case, there were competing applications for the child the subject of the proceeding to live in Country XX (the mother’s position) or Australia (the father’s position). The father filed an affidavit seeking to explain his rationale for consenting to certain orders to be made in Country XX. Written submissions referred to evidence that legal advice from the Country XX lawyers was the basis on which the father’s consent to those orders had been made. The trial judge considered that by putting in issue the nature and efficacy of the legal advice he received from the Country XX lawyers and attempting to explain the rationale for providing his consent to the Country XX orders on the basis of that legal advice, he had waived any privilege that subsisted in that advice. Whilst the primary judge’s orders were overturned on appeal, the general propositions just stated survived.
Presently, the state of the evidence and the case more generally before me is that:
(a) the applicant alleges that, despite his words and actions to the contrary at the time, the respondent harboured a secret desire to divorce her from a point well before she executed the financial agreement;

(b) the respondent alleges that he did not form an intention to divorce the applicant until after she had signed the financial agreement when he discovered her infidelity;

(c) the respondent had indeed had some thoughts about divorcing the applicant well before the financial agreement was signed as evidence by the correspondence with his lawyers in March 2021;

(d) in September 2021 the respondent instructed his Country B lawyers that he no longer wished to proceed with a divorce application against the applicant; and

(e) the divorce application commenced in Country B in late 2022 did not assert as a reason for the divorce the reason now advanced by the respondent.

However, all this means, is that the respondent has a credibility problem. Nowhere does he say that he told his lawyers about the real reasons for the divorce and for reasons unknown they omitted to include it in the divorce application. Nor does he put in issue any legal advice that he received from the Country B lawyers. Unlike the father in Sterling he does not say that he took a particular course of action because of legal advice, the nature and efficacy of which he now questions.
Unless and until the respondent puts the content of his communications in issue or makes an assertion that, in the sense described by Allsop J in DSE (Holdings) Pty Ltd, necessarily lays open the confidential communications to scrutiny, the content of his communications with his lawyers (as opposed to the fact of those communications) remains privileged and not open to scrutiny by the applicant. The issue enlivened by the respondent’s defence of the applicant’s claims does not lay open his instructions to his lawyers because the issue does not concern the content of those instructions. Moreover, the fact of those instructions has only peripheral relevance.
The applicant’s claim for production of the documents set out in paragraphs 2 and 3 of the Notice to Produce fails.

But the claim to inspect the advice from the Australian lawyers does not fail

The request to inspect the document described by the parties as item 4 is of a different nature. I have set out the email from the respondent to the applicant out above. In it, he discloses some of the advice from his Australian lawyer, but not all of it. The part he has disclosed refers to earlier paragraphs of the advice not disclosed.
The respondent referred me to Federal Treasury Enterprise (FKP) Sojuzplodoimport v Spirits International BV (No 6) [2019] FCA 337; (2019) 369 ALR 267 at [25] where Stewart J stated:
Different considerations may arise between circumstances where, for example, an assertion is made in a pleading which is said to have the effect of waiving privilege (sometimes referred to as issue waiver) and where, as another example, privilege is waived over part of a document or communication and not over other parts of it (sometimes referred to as partial disclosure). Those different considerations must, however, inform the assessment of whether there is inconsistency between the maintenance of the privilege and the relevant conduct of the party. One consideration in the case of partial disclosure is whether the disclosed and the undisclosed parts of the communication cover the same or different subject matters. That is because if they cover the same subject matter, disclosure of only part of the communication may lead to the meaning or import of the disclosed part being distorted or inaccurately perceived. Thus in the case of partial disclosure, the assessment of whether or not there is relevant inconsistency is likely to include a consideration of whether or not the disclosed and undisclosed parts cover different subject matter.
The respondent relies upon an affidavit from his solicitor to make good the argument that the whole of the relevant communication should not be produced for inspection by the applicant. He argues that those parts of the document that he has not disclosed deal with different subject matter to that dealt with in the disclosed portion of the letter.
However, whilst that might be so, the redacted portions are integral to a proper understanding of the disclosed portion as the words. So much is clear from the sentence “We do not advise that you adopt such approach, in view of the risk set out above”.
Whilst the respondent clearly intended to preserve the confidentiality of the balance of the letter by his use of the words, “I did not copy and am not sending you the previous paragraphs, as they are not relevant to your question.”, whether privilege has been lost is not a function of the subjective intention of the party said to have waived the privilege. It might be lost despite an intention to preserve it.
I consider that the respondent has clearly waived privilege to the whole of the communication. Here, the disclosure of a portion of the relevant communication, which is dependent upon other parts of the communication for a proper understanding of the disclosed portion, is clearly inconsistent with a maintenance of the confidentiality of the undisclosed portion of the document.

There should be no order as to costs

Both parties seek their costs of the application in the event of success on their side of the record. Neither has been entirely successful. The costs of this application should fall to each of the parties in accordance with the usual rule set out in s 114UB(1) of the Act. There will be no order as to costs.
